1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
99	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
106	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
120	  k\(-scheme \)C\( guarantees that both \)H^0\( and \)H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
127	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
128	  sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(C\) and returns an integer; the natural input
129	  shape is the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
130	  wrapper \(\HModule \bar k\) (REF) applied to a
131	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured carrier built from \(\mathcal F\), with
132	  \(\dim_{\bar k}\) extracted via \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\). The
133	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
134	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) and from the \(\Ext\)-based cohomology
135	  construction).
136	\end{definition}

138	\section{The \(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	
143	\leanok
144	[\(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor]
145	  \label{def:l_invariant}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
147	  
148	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\) and of the
149	  % "dimension of \(|D|\)") (read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
151	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote \(\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))\) by \(l(D)\),
152	  % so that the \emph{dimension of \(|D|\)} is \(l(D) - 1\). The number
153	  % \(l(D)\) is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
154	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\)).}
155	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let
156	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor (REF). The
157	  \emph{\(\ell\)-invariant of \(D\)} is the integer
158	  \[
159	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
160	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
161	  \]
162	  the \(\bar k\)-dimension of the space of global sections of the
163	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\). Finiteness of
164	  \(\ell(D)\) is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
165	  theorem on a proper \(\bar k\)-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
166	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system \(|D|\)} has
167	  \(\bar k\)-dimension \(\ell(D) - 1\) as a projective space, but the
168	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; \(\ell(D)\) is
169	  the only quantity it needs.
170	
171	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
172	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
173	  \(D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}\) (REF) and returns
174	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))\),
175	  computed through the same \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
176	  pipeline as REF. The associated invertible
177	  sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
178	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
179	  signature reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) by name and consume only its
180	  \(H^0\) functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}

183	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
184	
185	\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}
186	
187	The proof of REF below
188	decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
189	Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
190	(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
191	classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
192	of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
193	(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
194	elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
195	of sheaves; the third
196	(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
197	on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
198	Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
199	independently of the parent inductive argument.
200	
201	\begin{lemma}\leanok
202	[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
203	  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
205	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
206	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
207	  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
208	  % short exact sequence) (read from
209	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
211	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
212	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
214	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
215	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
216	  \(\bar k\), and let
217	  \[
218	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
219	  \]
220	  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
221	  sheaves on \(C\). Then
222	  \[
223	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
224	  \]
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
229	  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
230	  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
231	  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
232	  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
233	  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
234	  resulting nine-term sequence
235	  \[
236	    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
237	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
238	    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
239	  \]
240	  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
241	  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
242	  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
243	  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
244	  \[
245	    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
246	    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
247	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
248	  \]
249	  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
250	  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
251	  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
252	  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
253	  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
254	  \[
255	    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)
256	    - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_1) + \dim H^1(\mathcal F_2) - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_3)
257	    \;=\; 0,
258	  \]
259	  which rearranges, using the definition
260	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
261	  (REF), to
262	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
263	
264	  % NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness machinery.
265	  % Mathlib `b80f227` ships `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` but not (a)
266	  % the `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised to the
267	  % project's sheaf-cohomology pipeline `Scheme.HModule`, (b) Grothendieck
268	  % vanishing `H^i(C, F) = 0` for `i ≥ 2` at the `Ext`-cohomology level
269	  % consumed by `HModule`, nor (c) the six-term rank-counting bridge
270	  % composed of `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` invocations.
271	  % The Lean helper `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` therefore
272	  % carries a Tier-3 honest typed sorry; its closure lands once the
273	  % three substrate pieces are in place. Off the genus-0 critical path
274	  % (the parent theorem `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
275	  % is the only consumer in `RR.2`).
276	\end{proof}
277	
278	\begin{lemma}\leanok
279	[Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic]
280	  \label{lem:euler_char_iso}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso}
282	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
283	  \textit{Project-bespoke decomposition} (Archon-original packaging of
284	  the Mathlib \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; no external source named).
285	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
286	  \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F, \mathcal G\) be \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
287	  sheaves on \(C\) with an isomorphism \(\mathcal F \cong \mathcal G\).
288	  Then
289	  \[
290	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal G).
291	  \]
292	\end{lemma}
293	
294	\begin{proof}
295	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
296	  \leanok
297	  An isomorphism \(e \colon \mathcal F \xrightarrow{\;\cong\;} \mathcal G\)
298	  induces, in each cohomological degree \(n \in \mathbb N\), a
299	  \(\bar k\)-linear equivalence
300	  \[
301	    H^n(C, \mathcal F) \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; H^n(C, \mathcal G)
302	  \]
303	  by post-composition with the Ext-degree-zero classes
304	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{hom})\) and
305	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{inv})\). The mutual-inverse identities
306	  for this pair of post-compositions reduce — via the associativity
307	  lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.comp\_assoc\_of\_third\_deg\_zero}\), the
308	  composition lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0\mathtt{\_comp\_mk}_0\), and the
309	  iso identities \(e.\mathtt{hom} \circ e.\mathtt{inv} = \mathrm{id}\),
310	  \(e.\mathtt{inv} \circ e.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}\) — to the identity
311	  map on \(\Ext\) elements. Specialising to \(n = 0\) and \(n = 1\) and
312	  applying \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) at each of those two
313	  degrees gives
314	  \(\dim H^n(C, \mathcal F) = \dim H^n(C, \mathcal G)\) for
315	  \(n \in \{0, 1\}\). Unfolding the definition
316	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
317	  (REF) closes the goal.
318	\end{proof}

363	\begin{lemma}\leanok
364	[Euler characteristic of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf]
365	  \label{lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}

467	\subsection*{The main \(\chi\)-identity}
468	
469	\begin{theorem}
470	
471	
472	\leanok
473	[Euler-characteristic identity for \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)]
474	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
476	  
477	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
478	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any \(D\), $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
479	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
480	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
481	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
482	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
483	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
484	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
485	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
486	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
487	  % is the Euler characteristic
488	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
489	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
490	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
491	  \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). Then
492	  \[
493	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
494	  \]
495	\end{theorem}
496	
497	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case \(D = 0\). Then our
498	% formula says
499	% \(\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g\),
500	% because \(H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k\) for any projective variety
501	% (I, 3.4), and \(\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g\) by (1.1).
502	% Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point. We will show
503	% that the formula is true for \(D\) if and only if it is true for
504	% \(D + P\). Since any divisor can be reached from \(0\) in a finite number
505	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
506	% the result holds for all \(D\).
507	% We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its structure sheaf is
508	% the skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\), which we denote by
509	% \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\) by (II, 6.18).
510	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
511	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
512	% Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
513	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
514	% (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
515	% does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\)). Now the Euler characteristic is
516	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
517	% = 1$, so we have
518	% \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\).
519	% On the other hand, \(\deg D + P = \deg D + 1\), so our formula is true
520	% for \(D\) if and only if it is true for \(D + P\), as required."
521	\begin{proof}
522	  \leanok
523	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:l_invariant,
524	    lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
525	    lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
526	
527	  The proof is by induction on the structure of \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as the free
528	  abelian group on closed points (REF,
529	  REF). Both sides of the asserted
530	  equation are additive in \(D\) (the right-hand side by linearity of
531	  \(\deg\), REF; the left-hand side by the
532	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
533	  for \(D = 0\) and to show that it transports across the elementary
534	  modification \(D \mapsto D + [P]\) at any closed point \(P \in C\).
535	
536	  \emph{Base case \(D = 0\).} The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) is the
537	  structure sheaf \(\mathcal O_C\), and the formula
538	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\) reads
539	  \[
540	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
541	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
542	    \;=\; 1 - g.
543	  \]
544	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
545	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g\) (REF). The
546	  first term is \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\): \(C\) is a
547	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
548	  closed field \(\bar k\), so its connected components agree with its
549	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
550	  \cong \bar k\( as \)\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
551	  statement that ``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\) for any projective
552	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
553	  the project's \(H^0\)-bridge in
554	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
555	  two values gives \(1 - g = 1 - g\), as required.
556	
557	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)''
558	  step).} Let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
559	  argument: view \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(C\); its structure
560	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\) supported at \(P\),
561	  and its sheaf of ideals is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\)
562	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out \(P\) in \(C\)). Hence
563	  the short exact sequence
564	  \[
565	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
566	  \]
567	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
568	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) (a locally free
569	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module of rank \(1\), so tensoring preserves exactness
570	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged) yields
571	  \[
572	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
573	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
574	    \to k(P) \to 0.
575	  \]
576	  (Strictly: tensoring \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\) with
577	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) produces a sheaf isomorphic — but not
578	  syntactically equal — to \(\mathcal O_C(D)\), and the skyscraper
579	  factor \(k(P)\) is preserved up to isomorphism because
580	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank \(1\). We transport
581	  along this isomorphism via iso-invariance of \(\chi\),
582	  REF.)
583	  Additivity of \(\chi\) on the short exact sequence
584	  (REF, the project-side
585	  factoring of the ``Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
586	  exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1)'' input of Hartshorne~IV.1.3) gives
587	  \[
588	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
589	    \;=\;
590	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
591	  \]
592	  The skyscraper-sheaf input
593	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) is the standalone substrate lemma
594	  REF (factored from the
595	  same Hartshorne~IV.1.3 line); its proof splits into the
596	  H\(^0\) identification \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and the
597	  H\(^1\) vanishing \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\), pursued
598	  independently by the prover (see the lemma's two-paragraph proof
599	  body). Therefore
600	  \[
601	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
602	  \]
603	  On the other hand, \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
604	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), so by
605	  REF
606	  \[
607	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
608	  \]
609	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
610	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) holds for \(D\) if and only
611	  if it holds for \(D + [P]\). Since the closed-point divisors \([P]\)
612	  generate \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
613	  from \(D = 0\) to every \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) by induction on the (signed)
614	  number of generators in the expression of \(D\).
615	
616	  \emph{Lean reference note on the three substrate lemmas.} The
617	  inductive step of this proof consumes three substantive substrate
618	  lemmas, each pinned to its own Lean declaration in
619	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}: the additivity
620	  REF (\(\chi\) on a short
621	  exact sequence, gated on the project-side
622	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured Ext-LES carrier + Grothendieck
623	  vanishing + six-term rank-counting), the iso-invariance
624	  REF (\(\chi\) on
625	  isomorphism classes, axiom-clean against Mathlib's
626	  \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; closed iter-186), and the skyscraper
627	  identity REF
628	  (\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), splitting into an H\(^0\) half closed against
629	  the project's existing \(H^0\)-bridge and an H\(^1\) half gated on
630	  Mathlib flasque-cohomology — see the lemma's annotated proof body).
631	  Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} provides the Euler-characteristic API
632	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
633	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar},
634	  \texttt{GradedObject.eulerChar}) but does \emph{not} ship the
635	  specific additivity statement
636	  \texttt{CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar\_additive} at the
637	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf-cohomology level the project
638	  consumes; the additivity lemma is the project-side bridge.
639	\end{proof}

641	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
642	
643	\begin{theorem}
644	
645	
646	\leanok
647	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
648	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
649	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
650	  
651	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-\(0\) specialisation
652	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
653	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
654	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
655	  % if it is birational to \(\mathbf P^1\) (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
656	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
657	  % \(X\) is rational if and only if \(X \cong \mathbf P^1\) (I, 6.12). Now
658	  % using (1.3) we can show that \(X\) is rational if and only if \(g = 0\).
659	  % We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so
660	  % conversely suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two
661	  % distinct points of \(X\) and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
662	  % \(D = P - Q\). Since \(\deg K - D = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have
663	  % \(l(K - D) = 0\), and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of
664	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words $P \sim
665	  % Q\(. But this implies \)X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
666	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
667	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
668	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) with
669	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). Then
670	  \[
671	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
672	  \]
673	\end{theorem}
674	
675	\begin{proof}
676	  \leanok
677	  
678	  Specialising
679	  REF to the genus-\(0\)
680	  hypothesis \(g = 0\) yields
681	  \[
682	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
683	  \]
684	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF and the definition
685	  \(\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\)
686	  (REF), this rewrites as
687	  \[
688	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
689	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
690	  \]
691	  Under the standing genus-\(0\) hypothesis and the assumption
692	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\), the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
693	  \[
694	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
695	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
696	  \]
697	  Substituting yields \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\), as required.
698	
699	  \emph{The \(H^1\)-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
700	  irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), the vanishing
701	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) is the Hartshorne
702	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: \(\ell(D) \neq 0\) forces
703	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\); equivalently, for a curve where every
704	  degree-\(0\)-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
705	  sections after a single point-twist, \(H^1\) of the twist must vanish
706	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
707	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this \(H^1\)
708	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
709	  \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added), where the line
710	  bundle \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) and its cohomology are constructed; once

713	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
714	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
715	  \(H^1\)-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
716	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
717	  follow-up signature simplification.
718	
719	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
720	  this theorem is the genus-\(0\) classification
721	  REF of
722	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\)
723	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
724	  (REF), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
725	  produces a degree-\(1\) morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via

727	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\), which is then upgraded to an
728	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
729	\end{proof}

\chapter{Vanishing of \(H^1\) for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H\(^1\))}
\label{chap:RR_H1Vanishing}

% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.1, Exercise~1.16
% (flasque sheaves), Exercise~1.17 (skyscraper sheaves); III.2,
% Proposition~2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic) (read from
% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 67--68 and
% 225). All verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below were
% copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of the
% scanned PDF (no text layer is present).

\section{Setup and motivation}

This chapter is the project-side build of the
\texttt{RR.2.H\(^1\)} sub-phase, committed in iter-188 of the strategy
revision: \emph{the first cohomology of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf
on a smooth proper curve vanishes}. Concretely, the chapter pins the
identity
\[
  \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0
\]
on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\), where
\(P \in C\) is a closed point and the cohomology is computed through the
project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured \(\Ext\)-cohomology pipeline
\(\HModule \bar k\) (chapter~REF, sibling
\texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}).

\paragraph{Why a dedicated chapter.} Mathlib (snapshot
\texttt{b80f227}) does \emph{not} ship a flasque-cohomology vanishing
lemma at the \(\Ext\)-cohomology level used by
\(\HModule \bar k\), nor a Serre-duality theorem for curves. The
genus-\(0\) critical path nevertheless needs the skyscraper
\(H^1\)-vanishing twice: once inside the Hartshorne~IV.1.3
\(\chi\)-additivity assembly
(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, the parent lemma
\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf}), and once inside the
\texttt{RR.3} chain that produces a non-constant section
(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}, the downstream consumer
\texttt{h1\_vanishing\_genusZero}). The present chapter unifies the
substrate behind both consumers into a single short, self-contained
project-side build.

\paragraph{Strategy of the chapter.} The argument is the classical
Hartshorne~III~\S2 one: an injective resolution of a flasque sheaf
yields a flasque quotient at each stage (since both the injective object
and the original sheaf are flasque), and the global-sections functor is
exact on a short exact sequence of flasque sheaves. Iterating, every
derived functor of \(\Gamma\) vanishes on a flasque input in positive
degree. The chapter then specialises to the closed-point skyscraper
sheaf, which is flasque by inspection (it is the pushforward, along the
inclusion of a one-point closed subspace, of the constant sheaf on an
irreducible base — and pushforwards of flasque sheaves are flasque).

\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is
an algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signatures (planned location
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean}) carry the same
typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API
(\(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\, 1\),
\(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{IsIntegral}\)). The
flasque-vanishing lemma of \S2 below is stated more generally for a
noetherian topological space (the natural generality of
Hartshorne~III.2.5); the curve specialisation is read off by instance
synthesis on the underlying space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\).

\section{Flasque sheaves and their cohomology vanishing}
\label{sec:H1Vanishing_flasque}

\begin{definition}
[Flasque sheaf]
  \label{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition of flasque
  % sheaf, opening paragraph) (read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67); III.2,
  % paragraph preceding Lemma~2.4 (Hartshorne's own recall of the
  % definition) (PDF page 224).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Flasque Sheaves. A sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on a
  % topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
  % \(V \subseteq U\) of open sets, the restriction map
  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall (II, Ex. 1.16) that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on
  % a topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
  % of open sets \(V \subseteq U\), the restriction map
  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition); recalled
  III.2 preceding Lemma~2.4.}
  Let \((X, \mathcal O_X)\) be a ringed space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a
  sheaf of \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules on \(X\). We say that \(\mathcal F\)
  is \emph{flasque} if, for every inclusion of open sets
  \(V \subseteq U\) in \(X\), the restriction map
  \(\mathcal F(U) \to \mathcal F(V)\) is surjective.

  In the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured setup the predicate is
  applied to a sheaf
  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;J\;(\Module \bar k)\), where \(J\) is the
  Grothendieck topology of opens on the underlying topological space of
  the curve \(C\). The surjectivity condition is read on the underlying
  presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules; the project predicate
  \texttt{Scheme.IsFlasque} packages exactly this condition.

  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean predicate
  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque} takes a sheaf
  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
  (\Module \bar k)\) and asserts that for every pair \(V \le U\) of opens
  of \(X\), the restriction morphism
  \((\mathcal F.\mathtt{val.map}\;(\mathrm{leOp}\;V\;U)) :
  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;U) \to
  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;V)\) is surjective as a map of
  \(\bar k\)-modules.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque]
  \label{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward}
  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(d) (read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(f: X \to Y\) is a continuous map, and if
  % \(\mathscr{F}\) is a flasque sheaf on \(X\), then \(f_*\mathscr{F}\)
  % is a flasque sheaf on \(Y\)."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(d).}
  Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a continuous map of topological spaces and
  let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque sheaf on \(X\). Then the pushforward
  sheaf \(f_*\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(Y\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
  For an inclusion of opens \(V \subseteq U\) in \(Y\), the restriction
  map of \(f_*\mathcal F\) is by definition the restriction map of
  \(\mathcal F\) on the open sets \(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) of
  \(X\):
  \[
    (f_*\mathcal F)(U) \;=\; \mathcal F(f^{-1}(U))
    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal F(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U))\;}\;
    \mathcal F(f^{-1}(V)) \;=\; (f_*\mathcal F)(V).
  \]
  Since \(\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(X\), the right-hand restriction is
  surjective, hence so is the left-hand one. Therefore \(f_*\mathcal F\)
  is flasque on \(Y\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque]
  \label{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible}
  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(a) (read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Show that a constant sheaf on an irreducible
  % topological space is flasque. See (I, \S1) for irreducible
  % topological spaces."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(a).}
  Let \(X\) be an irreducible topological space and let \(A\) be a
  \(\bar k\)-module. Then the constant sheaf
  \((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)\;A\) on \(X\) is flasque.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
  On an irreducible topological space every non-empty open is connected
  and dense, so the constant presheaf
  \(U \mapsto A\) (with all non-empty restriction maps the identity)
  already satisfies the sheaf condition, and equals its sheafification.
  For every inclusion of non-empty opens \(V \subseteq U\), the
  restriction map is the identity on \(A\) — in particular surjective.
  For an inclusion involving the empty open, the target is the zero
  module, so surjectivity is immediate. Therefore the constant sheaf is
  flasque.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}
[Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology]
  \label{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero}
  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Proposition~2.5 (read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 225).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 2.5. \emph{If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a
  % flasque sheaf on a topological space \(X\), then
  % \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\).}"
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Proposition~2.5, p.~208.}
  Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque
  sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(X\), viewed as an object of
  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
  (\Module \bar k)\). Then, for every \(i \ge 1\),
  \[
    \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \;=\; 0.
  \]
  In particular, \(\dim_{\bar k} \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Embed \(\mathscr{F}\) in an injective
% object \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathfrak{Ab}(X)\) and let \(\mathscr{G}\)
% be the quotient:
% \(0 \to \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{I} \to \mathscr{G} \to 0\).
% Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque by hypothesis, \(\mathscr{I}\) is
% flasque by (2.4), and so \(\mathscr{G}\) is flasque by (II, Ex. 1.16c).
% Now since \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque, we have an exact sequence (II,
% Ex. 1.16b)
% \(0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{F}) \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{I}) \to
% \Gamma(X, \mathscr{G}) \to 0\).
% On the other hand, since \(\mathscr{I}\) is injective, we have
% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{I}) = 0\) for \(i > 0\) (1.1Ae). Thus from the long
% exact sequence of cohomology, we get \(H^1(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) and
% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) \cong H^{i-1}(X, \mathscr{G})\) for each
% \(i \geq 2\). But \(\mathscr{G}\) is also flasque, so by induction on
% \(i\) we get the result."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
  We follow Hartshorne's induction. Embed \(\mathcal F\) into an
  injective object \(\mathcal I\) of the abelian category
  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
  (\Module \bar k)\) — such an injective exists by the
  enough-injectives instance for sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on opens
  (\cref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK}). Form the quotient
  short exact sequence
  \[
    0 \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal I \to \mathcal G \to 0
  \]
  in the sheaf category.

  \emph{Step 1: the quotient \(\mathcal G\) is flasque.} The hypothesis
  gives that \(\mathcal F\) is flasque. The injective object
  \(\mathcal I\) is flasque by the Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4 input:
  any injective \(\mathcal O_X\)-module is flasque, where the
  \(\mathcal O_X\) here is the constant sheaf of rings \(\bar k\) on
  \(X\), so the sheaf category over the constant sheaf of rings is the
  category of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules and the lemma applies. From
  the two flasqueness inputs the quotient \(\mathcal G\) inherits
  flasqueness by Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(c): \emph{if
  \(0 \to \mathcal F' \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal F'' \to 0\) is an
  exact sequence and \(\mathcal F', \mathcal F\) are flasque, then
  \(\mathcal F''\) is flasque}.

  \emph{Step 2: the global-sections functor is exact on the chosen
  short exact sequence.} Since the leftmost term \(\mathcal F\) is
  flasque, the long exact sequence of cohomology starts with a short
  exact sequence on global sections
  (Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b)):
  \[
    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal F) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to
    \Gamma(X, \mathcal G) \to 0.
  \]

  \emph{Step 3: the long exact sequence gives the vanishing.}
  The object \(\mathcal I\) is injective in the abelian sheaf category,
  hence its right-derived global-sections cohomology vanishes in
  positive degree:
  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;i = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\). The long
  exact sequence of the short exact sequence above, applied to the
  derived functor \(\HModule\;\bar k\;{-}\;{\bullet}\) of \(\Gamma\),
  reads in degrees \(0\) and \(1\) as
  \[
    \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal G)
    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1
    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;1 = 0.
  \]
  The leftmost map is surjective by Step~2, so the connecting morphism
  to \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\) is the zero map, and the kernel
  of \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 \to 0\) is all of
  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\); hence
  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).

  \emph{Step 4: higher degrees by induction.} For \(i \ge 2\), the long
  exact sequence yields
  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \cong \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal G\;
  (i - 1)\); the inductive hypothesis on the flasque quotient
  \(\mathcal G\) gives the right-hand side is zero. This closes the
  vanishing in all positive degrees. The chapter consumes only the
  degree-\(1\) instance, but the lemma is stated and proved in full
  generality so it can support later sub-builds (e.g.\ Grothendieck
  vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme).

  % NOTE: the proof structure mirrors Hartshorne~III~Proposition~2.5
  % verbatim. The Lean closure is gated on three substrate inputs:
  % (a) the enough-injectives instance for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`
  % already provided by chapter
  % `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`; (b) the project-side
  % flasque-quotient lemma (Hartshorne~II~Ex.~1.16(c)) restated for
  % `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaves; (c) the long-exact-sequence
  % carrier for `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` at the `HModule kbar`
  % level (the same LES carrier consumed by
  % `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` in
  % `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). All three are project-side ~50-100
  % LOC apiece.
\end{proof}

\section{Skyscraper sheaves are flasque}
\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperFlasque}

\begin{lemma}
[Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward from a closed point]
  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.17 (read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 68).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Skyscraper Sheaves. Let \(X\) be a topological space,
  % let \(P\) be a point, and let \(A\) be an abelian group. Define a
  % sheaf \(i_P(A)\) on \(X\) as follows: \(i_P(A)(U) = A\) if \(P \in
  % U\), \(0\) otherwise. \ldots Show that this sheaf could also be
  % described as \(i_*(A)\), where \(A\) denotes the constant sheaf
  % \(A\) on the closed subspace \(\{P\}^-\), and \(i\colon \{P\}^- \to
  % X\) is the inclusion."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.17.}
  Let \(X\) be a topological space, let \(P \in X\) be a point with
  closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module.
  Write \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow X\) for the closed
  embedding. Then the skyscraper sheaf
  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A\) on \(X\) is naturally isomorphic to
  the pushforward \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;A\bigr)\)
  of the constant sheaf with value \(A\) on \(\overline{\{P\}}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  Both presheaves send an open \(U \subseteq X\) to \(A\) when
  \(P \in U\) (equivalently, when
  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}}\) is non-empty, since the closed subset
  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is irreducible and any open meeting it contains
  \(P\)), and to \(0\) when \(P \notin U\) (equivalently,
  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}} = \varnothing\)). The restriction maps agree
  by inspection: in the \(P \in V \subseteq U\) case both are the
  identity on \(A\); in the \(P \notin V\) case both factor through
  \(0\). The natural identification on opens is a sheaf morphism, and
  on stalks it is the identity (the stalk at any \(Q \neq P\) outside
  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is zero on both sides; the stalk at any point of
  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(A\) on both sides). Hence the comparison is
  an isomorphism of sheaves.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Closure of a closed point is irreducible]
  \label{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible}

  Let \(C\) be a Noetherian scheme (in particular the smooth proper
  curve \(C / \bar k\) of this chapter), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed
  point. Then the closure \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
  (trivially: a singleton is irreducible as a topological space).

  \textit{Project-bespoke ancillary} (immediate set-theoretic fact;
  no external source pinned).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  A singleton topological space is irreducible: its only non-empty open
  is the whole space, which trivially intersects every other non-empty
  open in a non-empty set (in fact in itself). For a closed point
  \(P \in C\) the closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(\{P\}\), which is
  homeomorphic to a singleton, hence irreducible.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque]
  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward,
        lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible,
        lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}

  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then the
  closed-point skyscraper sheaf
  \[
    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)
  \]
  is flasque as a sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(C\).

  \textit{Project-bespoke packaging} (no separately named external
  source; the constituent inputs are quoted in their own lemma blocks
  above).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward,
        lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible,
        lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
  By
  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, the skyscraper sheaf is
  isomorphic to the pushforward
  \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;
  (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr)\) along the inclusion
  \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow C\). The base
  \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
  (\cref{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}), so the constant sheaf on
  it is flasque (\cref{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}). Pushforward
  of a flasque sheaf is flasque
  (\cref{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}). Flasqueness transports along the
  isomorphism of \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, so
  \(k(P)\) is flasque.
\end{proof}

\section{\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes}
\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperH1}

\begin{theorem}
[\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf has dimension zero]
  \label{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}

  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
  \[
    \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0,
  \]
  where the left-hand side is the \(\bar k\)-finrank of
  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
  \bar k\;\bar k))\;1\).

  \textit{Project-bespoke assembly} of the two substrate inputs of this
  chapter (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} and
  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). The chapter's external source
  is Hartshorne~III.2 Proposition~2.5 applied to the flasque skyscraper
  sheaf — both inputs are quoted verbatim in their own lemma blocks
  above.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
  The closed-point skyscraper sheaf
  \(k(P) = \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;
  \bar k)\) is flasque (\cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). By the
  flasque-cohomology vanishing theorem
  (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}) applied at \(i = 1\) on the
  underlying topological space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\),
  \[
    \HModule\;\bar k\;(k(P))\;1 \;=\; 0.
  \]
  Taking \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\) of the zero module gives
  the claimed identity \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
\end{proof}

\section{Out of scope}

The following items lie outside this chapter and are scheduled into
sibling sub-builds:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{General coherent-sheaf cohomology vanishing.} Higher
    cohomology vanishing for general coherent sheaves on a curve
    requires Grothendieck vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme
    (Hartshorne~III.2.7), which the chapter does \emph{not} use; the
    chapter is narrowly tailored to the flasque/skyscraper case
    \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) that the genus-\(0\) critical path needs.
  \item \emph{Serre duality for curves.} The general-\(g\) Riemann--Roch
    statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) routes through
    Serre duality \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
    \mathcal L^{-1})^*\); Mathlib has neither the dualizing sheaf
    \(\omega_C\) on a scheme nor the duality theorem. The chapter
    sidesteps duality entirely by using flasqueness directly (the
    Hartshorne~III~\S2 order of presentation, not the
    Hartshorne~III~\S7 one).
  \item \emph{The \(H^0\) half of \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).} The
    one-dimensionality identification \(\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) is the
    sibling substrate lemma
    REF of
    \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, closed axiom-clean iter-188
    against the project's four-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain.
    The present chapter is the complementary H\(^1\) half.
